coredata.dc:title: Experimental studies of liquid immersion cooling for 18650 lithium-ion battery under different discharging conditions
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yang
Zhou, Zhifu
Hu, Leiming
Bai, Minli
Gao, Linsong
Li, Yulong
Liu, Xuanyu
Li, Yubai
Song, Yongchen
coredata.dc:description: In this study, fluorinated liquid immersion cooling as a new cooling scheme has been tested and discussed for cooling the 18650 lithium-ion battery (LIB). SF33, with the boiling point of 33.4 °C, is chosen as the liquid for the immersion cooling. Comparison of the SF33 immersion cooling and forced air cooling (FAC) for the 18650 LIB under 2C, 4C and dynamic load conditions are made. It is found that the immersion cooling better cools the battery under all these conditions. Under 4C discharging, the maximum cell temperature rise is 14.06 °C for the FAC, while is 4.97 °C for the SF33 immersion cooling. As the temperature of SF33 basically governs the cell temperature, the SF33 temperature should not be too low. This study demonstrates that the LIB has non-negligible power losses under SF33 temperature of 10 °C and 15 °C, compared with the temperatures above 20 °C. Lastly, the two-phase boiling heat transfer mechanisms associated with immersion cooling are discussed with bubble dynamics analysis. It is found under a higher C rate, more aggressive boiling heat transfer is induced. The battery temperature is controlled to be below 34.5 °C even under 7C discharging condition.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion battery
Thermal management
Immersion cooling
Pool boiling
Two-phase heat transfer

Temperature response under dynamic load conditions
The effect of different ambient temperatures
Bubble diagrams of different discharging depths under different C rate discharging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FAC system and (b) the arrangement of thermocouples.
The temperature response of the cell under 2C rate discharging with natural convection.
The (a) current input and voltage response and (b) temperature evolutions under dynamic load condition.
The influence of different ambient temperatures on voltage and temperature rise at (a) 1C rate and (b) 2C rate discharging.
The diagram of bubbles growth (a) at the top of cell and (b) overall.
The (a) temperature responses and (b) bubble diagrams of cylindrical LIB under 3C rate discharging.
The (a) temperature responses and (b) bubble diagrams of cylindrical LIB under 7C rate discharging.

head.ce:title: Experimental studies of liquid immersion cooling for 18650 lithium-ion battery under different discharging conditions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Ocean Energy Utilization and Energy Conservation of Ministry of Education, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian, 116023, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Carnegie Mellon University, Pittsburgh, 15213, USA
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Carnegie Mellon University, Pittsburgh 15213, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, fluorinated liquid immersion cooling as a new cooling scheme has been tested and discussed for cooling the 18650 lithium-ion battery (LIB). SF33, with the boiling point of 33.4 °C, is chosen as the liquid for the immersion cooling. Comparison of the SF33 immersion cooling and forced air cooling (FAC) for the 18650 LIB under 2C, 4C and dynamic load conditions are made. It is found that the immersion cooling better cools the battery under all these conditions. Under 4C discharging, the maximum cell temperature rise is 14.06 °C for the FAC, while is 4.97 °C for the SF33 immersion cooling. As the temperature of SF33 basically governs the cell temperature, the SF33 temperature should not be too low. This study demonstrates that the LIB has non-negligible power losses under SF33 temperature of 10 °C and 15 °C, compared with the temperatures above 20 °C. Lastly, the two-phase boiling heat transfer mechanisms associated with immersion cooling are discussed with bubble dynamics analysis. It is found under a higher C rate, more aggressive boiling heat transfer is induced. The battery temperature is controlled to be below 34.5 °C even under 7C discharging condition.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the pressure of energy shortage and environmental protection have prompted global attention to electric vehicles (EVs) and hybrid electric vehicles (HEVs) [1]. Power sources are the core components of EVs, and the common power sources currently include lead-acid batteries, nickel-hydrogen batteries and LIBs. Among them, lead-acid batteries have low specific energy and produce large amounts of lead emissions during processing and recycling, causing irreversible environmental damage. Compared with lead-acid batteries, nickel-metal hydride batteries have a relatively high specific energy. However, nickel-metal hydride batteries have the memory effect and suffer from a series of problems such as serious charging heat and poor fast charging performance. In contrast, LIBs have no memory effect and have significant advantages such as high energy density, long cycle life and low self-discharging rate, so LIBs have become the preferred power sources for EVs [2,3]. However, LIBs’ reliability, safety, as well as longevity are quite sensitive to temperature [4]. Pesaran [5] concluded that the optimal operating temperature range for LIBs is 25–40 °C and the maximum allowable temperature difference within the cell pack is 5 °C. Ramadass et al. [6] concluded from capacity decay analysis of cell that the battery capacity decayed by 70% after 500 cycles at 50 °C. However, LIB will generate a great deal of heat during the charging and discharging process, and if the heat dissipation is insufficient, it would lead to an increase of cell pack temperature and temperature non-uniformity, which will cause the capacity decay and performance degradation of the cell pack [7]. At the same time, the conditions of overheating can lead to various exothermic chain reactions within the LIB [8,9], the results can be thermal runaway behaviors of the battery, such as catastrophic battery fire and explosion [10].

Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop a battery thermal management system (BTMS) with strong capability of heat dissipation [11]. An effective BTMS can ensure the temperature balance in the battery pack and avoid performance degradation due to the inconsistency between batteries. At the same time, the BTMS can effectively dissipate heat when the battery temperature is high, and rapidly heat the battery when the battery temperature is low, thus ensuring the LIB is always within a safe and reasonable operating temperature range, which greatly improves the performance and cycle life of the LIB. The current common battery cooling schemes mainly include FAC [12,13], mini-channel plate liquid cooling [14–16], phase change materials (PCM) cooling [17,18], heat pipe cooling [19–21], and direct liquid cooling [22]. The FAC adopts air as the heat transfer medium to absorb the heat generated by the cell through convective heat transfer. The FAC system was widely adopted in early Japanese and Korean EVs due to its simple structure and low cost. However, due to the low heat dissipation efficiency of FAC and the poor temperature homogeneity within the cell pack, many scholars have made various optimizations and improvements. Xun et al. [23] changed the volume of the air channel and thus improved the heat dissipation efficiency of the cell pack, but increased the temperature non-uniformity within the cell pack. Chen et al. [24] optimized the flow paths of air, resulting in a 29% reduction in the maximum temperature difference between different cells. Yu et al. [25] designed an air-flow-integrated BTMS with dual flow channels which significantly improved the temperature uniformity within the cell pack. Mahamud et al. [26] designed a reciprocating FAC system, which reduced the maximum temperature difference of the cell pack by 4 °C. However, it is difficult for the FAC system to meet the requirements of BTMS under the conditions of high C rate (C rate is the measurement of the charge and discharge current with respect to its nominal capacity) discharging or high ambient temperature due to the low thermal conductivity of air and the great influence of external environment temperature [27].

Compared with air, the thermal conductivity of water at room temperature is dozens of times that of air, and the specific heat capacity of water is four times that of air, so the liquid flow has a better heat transfer effect than the airflow, it means that the mini-channel plate liquid cooling system has a relatively higher cooling efficiency [8]. Meanwhile, the overall structure of the mini-channel plate liquid cooling system is more compact which makes the energy density of the cell pack relatively higher [28]. Rao et al. [29] designed a liquid cooling system with a variable contact surface, which significantly improved the temperature uniformity of the cell pack compared to the cooling system with a constant contact surface. Jarrett et al. [30] optimized the liquid flow path of the liquid cooling system so that the average temperature of the cell pack as well as the liquid pressure drop were controlled within a reasonable range. Zhao et al. [31] designed a liquid-cooled cylinder based on microchannel cooling for cylindrical LIBs, which successfully limited the maximum temperature of the battery below 40 °C. Huang et al. [32] introduced the concept of streamlined shape into the design of microchannel cold plates, and the results showed that the use of streamline shape microchannel could increase the heat transfer efficiency by 44.52%, and at the same time, the temperature uniformity could be noticeably improved. Li et al. [33] investigated the effect of arrangements and channel design of cooling plates on the heat dissipation of batteries and found that the design with two cooling plates sandwiching one cell module showed better cooling performance. Akkaldevi et al. [34] created a two-way coupled transient electrochemical-thermal model for simulating the cooling of a 20 Ah LiFePO4 battery, the results showed that the cell temperature predicted by the model was in good agreement with the experimental data, and the model could be used for the thermal management of LIBS. In addition, the cloud-based smart battery management system (BMS) can be used for condition monitoring and fault prediction of the LIB system [35]. On this basis, replace the batteries which are in poor condition to prolong the lifespan of the packs [36]. Nowadays, mini-channel plate liquid cooling has become the preferred solution for commercial BTMS. Typical EVs in domestic and abroad, such as the BMW i3, Tesla, GM Volt and Geely Emgrand EVs all utilize mini-channel plate liquid cooling to control cell temperature. However, the mini-channel plate liquid cooling system requires a large number of components and the structure is relatively complex, in addition, the coolant is prone to leakage, which has a great safety hazard [37].

Unlike FAC and mini-channel plate liquid cooling, PCM cooling, which utilizes the latent heat of phase change materials to absorb and store the heat generated by the cell during operation, has gained much attention due to its simple structure and higher efficiency [38,39]. Al Hallaj et al. [40] first combined PCM with BTMS and showed that PCM cooling can significantly reduce the maximum temperature and temperature non-uniformity of cell pack. Choudhari et al. [41] investigated the thermal behavior of 5 × 5 cells configured battery pack under PCM cooling conditions using different fin structures by numerical simulation, providing new references for the design of fin structures in different cell pack systems. Paraffin has become the most commonly used PCM due to its high latent heat, non-toxicity, chemical stability, and low price [42]. However, the thermal conductivity of paraffin is relatively low, thus some scholars have added high thermal conductivity materials such as foam metal [43], expanded graphite [44], and carbon fiber [45,46] to paraffin to improve its thermal conductivity. Tian et al. [47] analyzed a stable composite phase change material composed of paraffin, expanded graphite and carbon fibers, the results show that the thermal conductivity can be increased to 2.09 W/m·K. Khateeb et al. [48] combined paraffin with aluminum foam, which resulted in a 50% reduction in cell maximum temperature. However, adopting PCM cooling can significantly reduce the energy density of the cell pack, while Mohammadian et al. [49] pointed out that when the phase change material is completely melted, it will not be able to continue to dissipate the heat of cell. As a thermal superconductor, heat pipe has the advantages of compact structure, flexible shape and long service life, which can be used for BTMS [50,51]. In practical battery thermal management applications, heat pipes usually need to be combined with other cooling schemes and thus provide superior cooling performance. Wu et al. [27] used heat pipes with aluminum fins to cool the cell pack, and research shows that inserting heat pipes in the cooling system could significantly reduce the battery temperature rise and maintain sound temperature uniformity. Rao et al. [52] investigated the BTMS based on PCM/oscillating heat pipe (OHP), and the results showed that the cooling scheme has an excellent cooling performance.

As an alternative battery thermal management scheme, direct liquid cooling adopts a dielectric liquid, which is in direct contact with the battery and utilizes sensible (single-phase cooling) or latent heat (two-phase cooling) of the liquid to take away the heat generated by the cell during operation, thus achieving the control of cell temperature. There is no thermal contact resistance between the cooling medium and the battery during the cooling process, thus direct liquid cooling has a higher cooling efficiency. Wang et al. [53] investigated the BTMS based on HFE-7000 direct flow boiling by means of experiments and numerical simulations. The results show that the two-phase heat transfer of HFE-7000 has a high heat dissipation capacity, and the maximum temperature as well as the maximum temperature difference of the cell pack does not exceed 38.37 °C and 3.71 °C, respectively. Al-Zareer et al. adopted numerical simulations to investigate the cooling performance of immersion cooling based on ammonia [54] and propane [55] respectively, the results showed that the immersion cooling system can maintain the cell pack temperature in the optimal range under high C rate discharging. Van Gils et al. [56] submerged a cylindrical LIB into HFE-7000 fluorinated liquid, and the experimental results proved that the immersion cooling scheme based on HFE-7000 has an excellent cooling capacity, even if the fluorinated fluid did not boil, it still showed an excellent heat dissipation effect. Du et al. [57] proposed a BTMS based on heat pipe and immersion cooling, which takes advantage of the boiling heat transfer of HFE-649 fluorinated liquid and the higher thermal conductivity of heat pipe to achieve the suppression for cell pack thermal runaway and stop the propagation and spread of thermal runaway.

In summary, it is clear that immersion cooling has a stronger heat dissipation capability than traditional FAC, liquid cooling and PCM cooling, significantly reduces the temperature rise of the battery under high C rate discharging, and the dielectric fluid used has favorable flame retardant properties, which greatly reduces the risk and effects of thermal runaway. However, the research on immersion cooling for LIB is still in the early stage, and the immersion cooling scheme has yet to be widely implemented industrially at scale, Meanwhile, the experimental studies on LIB immersion cooling are still relatively few. Therefore, this paper carried out a series of experimental investigations for the cooling performance of the immersion cooling based SF33 for the cylindrical LIB under different conditions. Firstly, the temperature responses of the cell under rapid discharging and dynamic load conditions under two cooling schemes, air-cooled and SF33 immersion cooling, are specifically compared. Then, the temperature response and voltage response of LIB at different SF33 temperatures were discussed to provide some meaningful reference for the design of appropriate temperature domain for SF33 when it is used for LIB immersion cooling. Till now, the analysis of the pool boiling process associated with liquid immersion battery cooling process and the boiling heat transfer mechanisms during the cooling of cylindrical LIB are still lacking. Thus, this paper utilized high-speed photographs to observe and record the gas-liquid phase change phenomena in the SF33 immersion cooling, and the physical mechanisms involved in the two-phase heat transfer process of SF33 were initially explored. The purpose of the study is to point out the potential advantage for the liquid immersion cooling in LIB cooling. In addition, the study aims to pave the way for the practical application of SF33 immersion cooling scheme in EVs as well as in the energy storage.

The battery adopted in this experiment is Sony VTC6 cylindrical lithium-ion battery, which is an 18650 power battery with as the positive electrode material, graphite as the negative electrode material and LiPF6 as the main component of electrolyte. The nominal voltage and nominal capacity of this high-energy lithium-ion cell are 3.65 V and 3.0 Ah, and the specific parameters of VTC6 are shown in Table 1 . The C rate presented in this work is based on the cell capacity of 3.0 Ah.

The SF33 engineered fluid (Chemours, USA) was selected as a working medium for the proposed cooling strategy. SF33 is a proprietary hydrofluoroolefin (HFO) specialty fluid with zero ozone depletion potential (ODP) and very low global warming potential (GWP) of 2100-yr ITH. It’s a clear, colorless, nonflammable, thermally stable, low toxicity, and environmentally friendly fluid developed for cleaning, cooling, and carrier fluid industrial solvent applications. The specific parameters of SF33 are shown in Table 2 .

As shown in Fig. 1 , SF33 immersion cooling system was established, it contains LIB, battery holder, glass container, temperature chamber (LX-80L), battery testing equipment (LAND CT6001A), computer, temperature data logger (Keysight, 34970A), fill light and high-speed camera. The battery testing equipment is used to control the charging and discharging of the battery and to monitor the current and voltage during the charging and discharging process in real-time. The temperature data logger is used to monitor the temperature of the battery and the fluorinated liquid in real-time and to transmit the temperature data to the computer. The temperature chamber is used to simulate different ambient temperatures in order to investigate the temperature response and power characteristics of LIB under different climatic conditions (hot regions in summer and cold regions in winter).

Before the experiment started, the selected LIB was placed in a room-temperature environment, discharged at 0.5C rate until the battery voltage reached the discharge cut-off voltage of 2.75 V and left to stand for 1 h to make the battery close to the equilibrium state. Then the LIB was charged at CC-CV mode, where the current in the CC stage is 1.5A (0.5C), and the charging is stopped when the battery current reaches the charging cut-off current.

When the LIB is subjected SF33 immersion cooling, the fully charged LIB was placed in the temperature chamber with temperature fluctuations of less than ±0.5 °C and set to the corresponding ambient temperature. The thermocouples were set in the middle of the battery and in the SF33 to measure the temperature response of cell and SF33. Started the experiment when the temperature of cell and SF33 stabilized at the corresponding ambient temperature. During the experiment, LIB discharged to the cut-off voltage in constant current (CC) mode. At the initial stage of discharging, the temperature of SF33 does not reach the boiling point, at this time, SF33 utilizes sensible heat to absorb the heat generated by the battery during the battery operation. As the discharging continues, the surface temperature of the battery gradually reaches the boiling point and above, and SF33 begins to boil, meanwhile, SF33 utilizes the latent heat to absorb the great deal of heat generated by the LIB.

The glass container used in this experiment has good compatibility with SF33, and at the same time, the glass container has excellent transparency, so that the gas-liquid phase change phenomena can be clearly observed during the SF33 immersion cooling. In order to better record and analyze, the 5KF10 high-speed camera which is made by Hefei Fuhuang Junda Hi-Tech Information Technology Co and Nikon AF MICRO NIKKOR 70–180 mm 1:4.5–5.6 D lens were used to photograph the phase change process. In this work, the shooting frame rate is set to 3333 fps. In addition, two EF-150 LED always-on lights were used as the light source for shooting in the experiment.

The schematic diagram of the FAC system is shown in Fig. 2 (a), which contains LIB, battery holder, blower, temperature chamber, temperature data logger, computer and battery testing equipment. Thermocouples are set in the middle of the LIB and at the air inlet to measure the temperature response of the LIB and the temperature of the incoming air. The LIB is installed on the battery holder and connected to the battery testing equipment via wires, thereby controlling the charging/discharging cycle of the LIB. The same as SF33 immersion cooling system, the battery is placed in the temperature chamber and set to the corresponding ambient temperature before the experiment started, meanwhile, the air inlet speed is set to 3 m/s by adjusting the blower power. During the experiment, the air flows through the surface of the battery and absorbs part of the heat generated by LIB, thereby lowering the temperature of cell.

As shown in Fig. 2 (b), the T-type thermocouple was used for this experiment, and the three thermocouples were placed at the top, middle and bottom positions of cell in order to have a clearer understanding of the temperature distribution at different positions. Since the upper part of the cylindrical LIB doesn’t have a winding layer, the distance between the temperature-monitoring point located above and the top of the battery is relatively larger. In order not to affect the heat transfer on the surface of battery and obtain the most realistic temperature distribution of battery surface, a 2 mm wide insulation tape is used to fix the thermocouple on the battery surface.

The major errors during this experiment come from the directly measured parameters, which include temperature errors, current errors, and voltage errors. The temperature error must be controlled within a pretty small range. Therefore, a standard J-type thermocouple is utilized to calibrate the T-type thermocouple in a constant temperature oil domain environment before the experiment started. It is found that the error between two types of thermocouples is within 0.1 °C in the temperature range of -20-80 °C. The minimum surface temperature of battery is 10 °C, so the maximum relative error of the surface temperature is:
(1)
ς
T w
=
δ
T w
T w
=
[
80 −
( − 20 )
]
× 0.1 %
50
= 0.2 %

According to the parameter introduction of the LAND battery testing equipment, the maximum current allowed by the equipment is 30A, and the test accuracy is 0.1%. The minimum current used in this experiment is 3A, so the maximum relative error is:
(2)
ς I =
δ I
I
=
30 × 0.1 %
3
= 1 %

According to the parameter specification of the LAND battery testing equipment, the maximum voltage allowed by the equipment is 5 V, and the test accuracy is 0.1%. The minimum voltage used in this experiment is 2.75 V, so the maximum relative error is:
(3)
ς U =
δ U
U
=
5 × 0.1 %
2.75
= 0.18 %
To sum up, the measurement error of this experiment is relatively small and has high accuracy and credibility.

Uniform temperature distribution is a key factor to ensure the efficient operation of LIB. According to Arrhenius law, the electrochemical reaction rate will increase exponentially with the increase of temperature [58]. Thus, it is essential to explore the surface temperature distribution of cylindrical cell before investigating the thermal performance of the two cooling schemes. Fig. 3 shows the temperature response of cell with 2C rate discharging under the condition of natural convection. It can be seen that the maximum temperature difference between the three temperature-monitoring points is always below 0.8 °C during the entire discharge process. This is due to the 18650 type LIB has a smaller volume and its shell is made of stainless steel, so that the surface temperature distribution of cell is uniform even under the weaker cooling method of natural convection. As mentioned by Kong et al. [59], the temperature difference between the top, middle and bottom of the cylindrical LIB is slight, and the temperature of the battery in the middle can represent the overall battery temperature. Therefore, the temperature difference of the single cell would not be explored in the subsequent experiments, and the thermocouple which located in the middle of cell was chosen to study the transient temperature response of battery under high C rate discharging and dynamic load test.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of different C rate discharging
ce:section.ce:para: The temperature response of battery with two cooling schemes, FAC and SF33 immersion cooling, are studied at 2C and 4C rate discharging when the ambient temperature is about 30 °C. The temperature response of battery under 2C rate discharging is shown in Fig. 4 . It is found that when the battery adopts SF33 immersion cooling, the surface temperature of the battery is basically lower than the boiling point of SF33 during the entire test. Due to the low thermal conductivity of the liquid SF33 and the weak flow of natural convection, the heat transfer coefficient of the single-phase heat transfer of the liquid SF33 is relatively low. The peak temperature of the cell during the entire test is 33.5 °C, with a temperature rise of approximately 3.02 °C, meanwhile, the temperature of SF33 gradually increases from 30.42 °C to 32.66 °C. When the battery adopts FAC, the temperature rise rate of the battery is slightly higher than that of SF33 immersion cooling. It is clear that at the end of discharge, the battery temperature increased significantly, which is due to the rapid increase in the rate of heat production within the cell near the end of discharge [60]. The peak temperature of the cell during the entire test is 35.3 °C, with a temperature rise of approximately 4.95 °C. Therefore, the battery temperature is always controlled within a safe and reasonable range, which means that the FAC scheme can meet the requirements of battery thermal management at a low discharging rate.

The temperature response of cell under 4C rate discharging is shown in Fig. 5 . It can be seen that when the cell adopts FAC, the temperature rise of battery under 4C rate discharging is more intense than 2C rate discharging and the maximum temperature of battery during the whole discharging process is 44.45 °C, with a temperature rise of about 14.06 °C. The time of cell temperature exceeding 40 °C accounted for 51.4% of the entire discharging process. As described by Wu et al., it is hard for FAC to meet the requirements of battery thermal management under high C rate discharging [27]. However, when the cell adopts SF33 immersion cooling, the temperature of cell surface reaches the boiling point of SF33 at about 220s, at which time the liquid SF33 gradually began to boil. Compared with FAC, the immersion liquid cooling based on two-phase heat transfer has a much higher heat transfer efficiency [8]. As a result, the maximum temperature of cell is basically stable at 33–34 °C, and the maximum temperature rise of the battery during test is 4.97 °C, which is only 35.35% of that under FAC condition. It can be seen that SF33 immersion cooling scheme could effectively absorb the large amount of heat generated by the cell battery during high C rate discharging and maintain the cell temperature in the optimal temperature range. As the research done in section 4.2 below, the SF33 immersion cooling scheme meets the cooling demand under even the 7C discharging condition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Temperature response under dynamic load conditions
ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, it is common to use static test methods to evaluate the performance of batteries, which means using constant C rate for charging and discharging tests. However, for many practical applications, there are momentarily large current shocks and instantaneous current switching, such as Urban Dynamometer Driving Schedule (UDDS) test [61] and Dynamic Stress Test (DST) test [62]. So it is necessary to explore the temperature response of cell under dynamic load conditions.

This study conducted a simplified square wave test on the battery. The fully charged battery is discharged at 4C for 190 s and then left to stand for 190 s, so discharged and left to stand for 3 cycles, the voltage and current responses are shown in Fig. 6 (a). The 4C peak discharging simulates high power operation for short periods of time, such as starting, climbing and accelerating. Battery discharged from the initial 100% state of charge (SOC) to 39.87% SOC during the entire dynamic load test. The temperature response throughout the test is shown in Fig. 6 (b), it can be seen that the maximum temperature of the battery under FAC reaches 38.29 °C during the test, while the maximum temperature of cell under SF33 immersion cooling is only 34.29 °C. Since the current will fluctuate during the entire test, the heat generated will also change over time. It is found that during the whole dynamic load test, the temperature fluctuation of the battery under FAC condition is larger, about 4 °C, while the temperature fluctuation of the battery under SF33 immersion cooling is relatively small, about 1 °C, which is only about 25% of that under FAC condition.

Therefore, it can be seen that one of the major advantages of SF33 immersion cooling is its ability to reduce the control requirements of active cooling. At the same time, SF33 immersion cooling is a more passive cooling method, that is, SF33 immersion cooling can ensure that the temperature fluctuation of LIB operation is small even under unpredictable actual dynamic operating conditions of EVs, which greatly reduces the difficulty of design for cell cooling.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of different ambient temperatures
ce:section.ce:para: For LIBs under real operating conditions, it is critical to design their operating temperature range. The excessive operating temperature will increase the impedance of LIBs and accelerate the aging of LIBs [63]. At the same time, a lower operating temperature will lead to poor electrolyte conductivity and sluggish reaction kinetics of LIBs thereby leading to difficulties in discharging [64,65]. For the operation of EVs, the external ambient temperature is strikingly different, i.e. it needs to be considered for operation in hot regions in summer and cold regions in winter conditions. Therefore, the operating temperature of the battery in EV should not be designed too narrow. Cooling cell to 30 °C in summer already requires a large cooling power consumption, while heating a LIB to 15 °C in winter is more difficult and requires heating power consumption. When SF33 is selected for cooling, the battery is submerged in SF33, and the temperature of SF33 largely determines the operating temperature of the LIB. Therefore, the discussion on the surface temperature response of LIBs and the discharging performance of LIBs under different temperatures of SF33 has important practical significance.

In fact, the power and current of the LIB are dynamic during the operation of the EVs. This part of the research uses fixed 1C and 2C rate discharging to simplify the problem. At the same time, the temperature and discharging response of the LIB at different temperatures of SF33 are discussed. 1C and 2C rate discharging were chosen on account of the overall discharging rate is relatively slow when the EV is running in the actual process. Therefore, the result of 1C rate or less is already representative. When the battery was discharged at small C rate, the discharging curve of the battery, immersed in SF33, is the same as the curve that characterizes the battery performance under constant temperature conditions, so there is no need to make a separate discussion. However, 2C rate discharging corresponds to the extremely large power output of EVs in a short period of time. As shown in Fig. 7 (a), The temperature of LIB is basically determined by the temperature of SF33. When the battery is discharged at 2C rate, the temperature of SF33 will increase due to the heat generated by the cell. This is because when the environment at a low temperature, SF33 mainly relies on its own sensible heat to absorb the heat generated by the battery during the discharging, coupled with the thermal conductivity of the liquid SF33 is low, so the temperature rise rate is larger. That is, under cold operating conditions in winter, SF33 can absorb and store a certain amount of heat generated by the cell to maintain the battery's operating temperature, but it still needs external heating in general. When the ambient temperature is high, SF33 absorbs the heat generated by the battery so that its temperature rises quickly to the boiling point and above. At this time, SF33 uses latent heat to absorb the heat generated by the battery under high C rate discharging condition, so the temperature rise rate is greatly reduced.

During the discharging process, the cell voltage with low battery temperature is always lower than that with high battery temperature. The battery voltage curve at 10 °C is about 0.1 V lower than the battery voltage at 30 °C on average. A lower temperature will always result in a lower discharging power than the battery which in higher temperature, and at the same time it will also reduce the discharging efficiency and performance of battery [64]. On the whole, when the battery is discharged at a low rate of 1C and the initial temperature of SF33 is between 20 °C and 30 °C, it can ensure that the battery is always within an optimal temperature range during the whole discharging process. However, When the initial temperature of SF33 is 10 °C or 15 °C, the maximum temperature of the battery is still lower than 20 °C at the end of the discharging, and the discharging voltage is decreased to some extent. Then the battery is discharged at a higher rate of 2C. As shown in Fig. 7 (b), when the initial temperature of SF33 is between 20 °C and 30 °C, it can ensure that the battery is always within an optimal temperature range during the whole discharging process. However, when the initial temperature of SF33 is 15 °C, the maximum temperature of the battery exceeds 20 °C at the end of the discharging, only 50.48% of the discharging time that the cell can be kept at an optimal temperature range during the entire discharging process. Therefore, the initial temperature of SF33 between 20 °C and 30 °C can always maintain the battery's operating temperature within an optimal temperature range compared to 10 °C and 15 °C. However, for the fluorinated fluid temperatures of 10 °C or even below, a more in-depth discussion is needed on the balance and optimization between the power consumption and energy balance for heating the battery at low ambient temperature.

This section uses the high-speed photography to investigate the gas-liquid phase change phenomenon in the process of SF33 immersion cooling, focusing on the location of bubble growth as well as the growth process. Since the rough surfaces and crevice areas are easy to form the gasification core [66], the bubbles are mainly generated at the connection between the wire and the positive/negative terminal as well as the position of safety valve. The diagram of bubbles’ growth at the top of cell is shown in Fig. 8 (a). The whole group of images is time-oriented, recording the generation, growth, separation, rise and convergence of bubbles. As shown in state A in Fig. 8 (a), the bubble is generated at the top of the cell at the initial moment of recording. With the continuous heat production of the battery, the bubble volume gradually increases, and as shown in state C, the bubble approaches detachment. As shown in state D, the bubble has detached from the top of cell. However, the detachment of the bubble is not complete, leaving a small part of the bubbles to become the next active vaporization core. As shown in state H, the detached bubble is merging with the surrounding bubble during the ascending process to form a larger bubble and continue to rise.

The diagram of bubbles growth overall for the cell is shown in Fig. 8 (b). As shown in state A, the bubble is generated at the bottom of the battery at the initial moment. With the continuous heat production of the battery, the bubble volume gradually increases. As shown in state E, the bubble is on the verge of detachment. At the same time, the bubble is connected to a number of vaporization cores which located on the bottom of the battery via a column of gas. As the volume of the bubble continues to increase, the bubbles start to detach gradually. Similar to the phenomenon seen in Fig. 8 (a), some of the detached bubbles will merge with the surrounding bubbles in the process of rising, and eventually form larger bubbles to rise to the surface of the liquid. The detachment of the bubbles tears the stagnant liquid thermal boundary layer near the surface of the battery, causing a strong disturbance of the liquid and thus intensifying convective heat transfer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bubble diagrams of different discharging depths under different C rate discharging
ce:section.ce:para: In this section, the temperature response and the bubble characterization are investigated at different C rate discharging, among them, the bubble characterization under four different depths of discharge is compared under each discharging rate. Such as 3C rate discharging in Fig. 9 , the four points A, B, C and D in the figure correspond to the four discharging depth states of 25% depth of discharge (DOD), 50% DOD, 75% DOD and 100% DOD, respectively.

The temperature response at 3C rate discharging is shown in Fig. 9 (a), it is found that the temperature of SF33 is always below the boiling point during the whole process of 3C rate discharging. Therefore, the phenomenon of gas-liquid phase change generated during 3C rate discharging belongs to subcooled boiling. The bubble characterization at 3C discharging rate is shown in Fig. 9 (b), it is found that although the surface temperature of cell has not yet exceeded the boiling point of SF33, there are already a few small bubbles generated at 25% DOD. This is due to that the higher local resistance at the welding of the nickel sheet and the positive/negative terminal. The higher local resistance would generate more heat, and at the same time, such positions are easier to form gasification core. As the discharging process continues, the temperature of cell surface has exceeded the boiling point of SF33 at 50% DOD. At which time the SF33 near the surface of cell has begun to subcooled boiling. It can be seen from state B in Fig. 9 (b) that the detachment frequency of small bubbles increases slightly. The temperature of SF33 gradually approaches boiling point at 75% DOD as well as 100% DOD, and it is clear that the detachment frequency of bubbles gradually increases, which contains not only some small bubbles but also a few large bubbles. The highest temperature of batter is controlled to be below 34 °C during the whole discharging process.

The temperature responses and bubble diagrams at 7C rate discharging are shown in Fig. 10 , it is found that the discharging current is constant at 21A at the initial stage, and as the discharging process continues, the current gradually decreases due to the discharging limit of cell has been reached. The temperature rise of cell and SF33 is very drastic due to the higher discharging current. The temperature of cell surface exceeded the boiling point of SF33 at 108s, and at this time, the SF33 near the surface of cell begins to subcooled boiling. With the continuous heat production of cell, the temperature of SF33 gradually exceeds boiling point, and the heat transfer mode is mainly transformed into saturated boiling. Due to the extremely high heat transfer coefficient of SF33 immersion cooling through the direct contact of the batteries with the fluorinated fluid, especially the two-phase heat transfer [67], the temperature of cell is basically controlled below 34.5 °C even under ultra-high rate discharging. As shown in state A in Fig. 10 (b), the SF33 near the surface of cell is in the state of subcooled boiling and there are a large number of large bubbles generated at the welding of the nickel sheet and the positive/negative terminal. When the discharging process reaches to the states B, C and D as shown in Fig. 10 (b), the bubble detachment frequency is high and the volume of bubble is relatively larger. The higher discharging rate means higher heat production rate, and higher bubble generation rate means more phase-change heat transfer together with stronger two-phase heat transfer phenomenon. Thus, it can be concluded that the cooling capability of SF33 immersion cooling system increases with the increase of discharging rate. The 7C continuous discharging rate is already very extreme condition for normal LIB operating. And the present study points out that the liquid immersion cooling gives sufficient cooling for 18650 battery under extreme fast discharging condition.

This study investigates the SF33 immersion cooling scheme and thermal management of 18650 LIB under high C rate discharging conditions based on experimental tests. Meanwhile, the high-speed camera was used to record the bubble behavior at different depths of discharging to reveal the two-phase heat transfer mechanisms of SF33 immersion cooling for 18650 type LIB. The following conclusions can be drawn.
(1)
For cooling a SONY VTC6 18650 LIB, the SF33 immersion cooling approach provides higher heat removal capability compared with FAC under both continuous and dynamic discharging condition. Under 4C discharging rate, the FAC method results in a cell temperature rise of 14.06 °C, while the SF33 immersion cooling leads to a cell temperature rise only 4.97 °C. Meanwhile, the SF33 immersion cooling also shows a potential advantage in suppressing the temperature fluctuations of LIB under dynamic load condition without extensive active temperature control design.
(2)
The goal for adopting the liquid immersion cooling is to regulate the temperature of LIB under working conditions. To achieve this goal, the appropriate operating temperature range for the liquid coolant is explored. It is found that as the liquid immersion cooling provides high heat transfer coefficient with natural convection even without boiling. So the liquid temperature can govern the battery temperature. As a result, when the liquid temperature is 10 °C or 15 °C, the LIB would suffer non-negligible voltage loss under 1C and 2C discharging conditions. The present work only points out that too low temperature of the immersion liquid is inappropriate. Whether the liquid immersion system is suitable for the battery heating and battery pack heat preservation under cold condition still needs further study.
(3)
The gas-liquid phase change of SF33 occurred in the process of immersion cooling, from single-phase heat transfer to two-phase heat transfer, which greatly increases the heat transfer coefficient. The LIB temperature is suppressed to be below 34.5 °C even under 7C rate discharging. At the same time, the frequency of bubble detachment and the average diameter of the bubbles increase with the improvement of charging rate. The higher frequency of bubble detachment and larger average bubble diameter means more phase-change heat transfer and greater heat dissipation capacity, i.e. improving the temperature uniformity of the lithium-ion battery on the time scale using passive control. Whether the liquid immersion cooling can mitigate the LIB thermal runaway is an interesting topic for future research explorations.

Yang Li: Methodology, Investigation, Data analysis, Writing original draft, Visualization. Zhifu Zhou: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing-Reviewing and Editing. Leiming Hu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing-Reviewing and Editing. Minli Bai: Writing-Reviewing and Editing, Funding acquisition, Resources. Linsong Gao: Investigation, Visualization, Writing-Reviewing and Editing. Yulong Li: Investigation, Data analysis. Xuanyu Liu: Investigation, Visualization. Yubai Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Yongchen Song: Conceptualization, Supervision, Resources, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 52106226, 51876027, 52176058) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities, China (DUT20RC(3)095, DUT20JC21).
